quiz_title: AL — Introduction - Physical Culture: Historical Background [EN]
description: The historical roots of physical culture as an introduction to training theory: the origins of sport, its social functions, and cultural embeddedness.

questions.text: According to the text, why is it necessary to review the historical background of physical culture in order to understand the topic of fitness?
questions.explanation: The text states that understanding fitness and the fitness recreation movement requires first examining the historical background of physical culture and several problems of civilisational development.

options.text: Because the rules of individual sports can only be learned this way
Because understanding fitness as a global phenomenon requires historical and conceptual context
Because the text applies an exclusively historiographical approach
Because modern sport has no connection to earlier physical culture

questions.text: According to the chapter, the introduction is actually more of a lead-in to the topic rather than a mere short preface.
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that this chapter is more than an introduction — it is intended as a section that leads into the topic.

questions.text: According to the text, which was one of the earliest functions of sport?
questions.explanation: The text states that sport initially served as a means of preparation for hunting, and gradually for warfare.

questions.text: In the historical development of sport's function, what followed preparation for hunting?
questions.explanation: The text clearly indicates the sequence: first preparation for hunting, then gradually preparation for warfare.

questions.text: In ancient initiation rites, young people had to demonstrate their ability to hunt for survival and to defend their community.
questions.explanation: According to the text, in these trials the young person had to prove fitness for hunting and for defending their family, tribe, or people.

questions.text: According to the text, what are sports originally derived from?
questions.explanation: The authors state that sports are originally derived from ancient initiation rites.

options.text: The leisure customs of industrial society

questions.text: Which summary best reflects the historical role of athletic competitions according to the subchapter?
questions.explanation: The text states that athletic competitions throughout human history were instruments of preparation for work and/or warfare.

options.text: They primarily served as substitutes for religious ceremonies
They were instruments of preparation for work and/or warfare
They exclusively served the representation of rulers
They always served purely entertainment purposes

questions.text: Which civilisational context does the text present as the classical continuation of European physical culture?
questions.explanation: The authors state that the classical continuation of all this in European culture was found in Ancient Greece.

questions.text: According to the text, in Sparta the entire way of life of young men was subordinated to their military training and combat preparation.
questions.explanation: The subchapter emphasises that in Sparta, the entire way of life of young men was subordinated to their military training and combat preparation.

questions.text: Which statement most accurately expresses the essence of the passage about Spartan education?
questions.explanation: The core message of the text is that in Sparta, society treated military fitness as a central value and organised young men's way of life accordingly.

options.text: The primary goal of education was the development of artistic skills
Military fitness and combat preparation were defining social priorities
Physical education was entirely separated from social life
Physical exercise was given a role only on ceremonial occasions

questions.text: According to the text, the modern Olympic Games cannot be understood without ancient Greek culture.
questions.explanation: The authors explicitly state that the modern Olympic Games cannot be understood without ancient Greek culture.

questions.text: Which medieval form of movement does the text highlight as serving combat preparation?
questions.explanation: The text states that in the Middle Ages, knightly or chivalric tournaments — popular public events serving combat preparation — were also present.

questions.text: According to the text, playful forms of movement also featured in medieval festive celebrations.
questions.explanation: The authors clearly indicate that playful forms of movement also appeared in medieval festive celebrations.

questions.text: Which connection does this subchapter emphasise most strongly regarding the early history of sport?
questions.explanation: The main message of the subchapter is that early forms of sport served practical, social, and defensive functions — not merely entertainment.

options.text: Early forms of sport were primarily aimed at economic gain
Early forms of sport were closely linked to subsistence, labour, and warfare
The development of sport was independent of social needs
Sport served recreational purposes from the very beginning

questions.text: Which answer most accurately summarises the main idea of the 'Physical Culture: Historical Background' subchapter?
questions.explanation: The subchapter demonstrates that the historical roots of sport lie in hunting, combat preparation, initiation rites, and socially determined movement culture.

options.text: The history of sport essentially begins with the emergence of modern competitive sport
The historical roots of sport are linked to survival, defence, community fitness, and social functions
The development of physical culture was shaped exclusively by the Olympic movement
Sport has fulfilled the same function in every era
